a|true|high20840.txt.passage:
(1) From March 29 to April 19, a new version of Death of a Salesman was performedat tne Capital Theatre as the first in a series   of foreign classics to mark the 60th anniversary of the Beijing People's Art Theatre.
(2) The last time this play was performed in Beijing was 29 years ago.
(3) Death of a Salesman was created in 1949 by Arthur Miller, who  used it to reveal   the weakness of the "American Dream" .
(4) The main character, Willy Loman, believes wholeheartedly in American capitalism, and desires to "succeed" but his own greed destroys him in the end.
(5) Death of a Salesman's first performance was a huge hit on Broadway, where it shocked the American theatre world, and strengthened the 33-year-old Miller's status as a master of American theatre.
(6) The play won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama, New York Drama Critics' Circle, and the Tony Award for Best Play.
(7) The New York Times praised Death of a Salesman as a milestone of 20th century theatre.
(8) In 1999, Death of a Salesman received another Tony Award for Best Revival, and the then 83-year-old Miller was given the National Medal of Arts.
(9) The 1983 version of Death of a Salesman was directed personally by Arthur Miller on a visit to China, with Beijing director and actor Ying Ruocheng as the main character of Willy Loman.
(10) On May 7, 1983, the play was performed for the first time at the Capital Theatre, and the first series of performances lasted until August 18, with over 50 performances, some of which filled the house, and all of which had great influence.
(11) When it was first performed here, China didn't have "salesman" , so the actors had to use their imagination to represent the characters and American society, and the audiences weren't entirely sure about some information.
(12) For example, they couldn't understand what "fixed payments" were, or why, if Willy was a lower-class member of American society, his family had a house, car, refrigerator, and television.
(13) But this didn't stop the play from becoming a widely-known page in Beijing's theatre history.

(2) But recently big changes have seen these traditional publications try to fit the modern world.
(3) After 221 years, The Times has changed its size to become much smaller.

(5) In prefix = st1 /Britainthe newspaper market is divided between the larger broadsheets and the smaller tabloids.
(6) These terms refer to the size of the papers' pages but there is also a clear difference in content.
(7) Broadsheets such as The Times, the Guardian and Daily Telegraph are serious papers.
(8) They cover a broad range of political, economic and international issues.
(9) Their stories are also reasonably long and use quite formal language.
(10) Tabloids have far more stories about less serious issues such as celebrities' love lives.
(11) Their stories are shorter and use more simple language.
(12) Tabloids often have bigger pictures.
(13) Britain's best-selling newspaper, the Sun, is a tabloid and has a naked girl on Page Three every day.
(14) By changing to the size of a tabloid, The Times is following in the footsteps of a less famous broadsheet paper the Independent .
(15) It changed to tabloid last year and saw its sales increase greatly.
(16) Although both papers have switched to the smaller size the content of the papers has remained the same.
(17) They are both still serious papers.
(18) The two papers claim that people find the smaller size easier to handle when they travel to work on the bus or the train in the morning.
(19) Instead of calling the new style of their paper tabloid, the paper says its new size is "compact" .

(4) The city is a major tourist destination for both domestic and overseas visitors.
(5) London hosted the 1908 and 1948 Summer Olympic Games and will host the 2012 Summer Olympic Games.
(6) London contains four World Heritage Sites: the Tower of London, the historic settlement of Greenwich ;the Royal Botanic Garden, Kew; and the site comprising the Palace of Westminster, Westminster Abbey and St.Margaret 's Church.
(7) As of July 2007, it had an official population of 7,556, 900 within the boundaries of Greater London making it the most populous municipality in the European Union.
(8) Since the 2001 the Greater London Urban Area has been the second largest in the EU after Paris with a population of 8,278,251, and the metropolitan area is estimated to have a total population of between 12 million and 13 million, the largest population area in the EU.
(9) The public transport network, administered by Transport for London, is the most intensive in the world.
(10) Heathrow Airport is the busiest airport in the world by international passenger traffic and the air space is the busiest of any city in the world.

(2) Americans debate whether the drinking age should be lowered, or whether young drinkers would only drink more.
(3) In parts of Europe, the legal drinking age for beer, and sometimes hard liquor, is sixteen.
(4) Yet France may raise the age limit for beer and wine sales to eighteen, the same as for hard liquor there.
(5) Rules on alcohol differ from college to college in the United States.
(6) Many schools require all first-year students to take an alcohol prevention and education program, often given online.
(7) Some have a "zero tolerance" policy where alcohol is banned from all buildings.
(8) Parents are informed of violations  and students may be suspended .

(10) But alcohol is banned in first-year dorms --- where most students are under twenty-one anyway.
(11) Susan Davis, a university lawyer, says campus police and local police report underage drinking violations to administrators.
(12) The university judicial committee decides punishment on a case by case basis.
(13) For example, the committee might suspend or expel a student.
(14) It might require an alcohol education program.
(15) Or it might just give a warning.
(16) Jon Zug is a prosecutor  in Albemarle County, where the university is located.
(17) He says international students would face the same punishment as American citizens for underage drinking in Virginia.
(18) That includes a fine of five hundred dollars or fifty hours of community service.
(19) But first offenders might be given a chance to complete an alcohol education program instead.
(20) Schools have to report legal violations by international students to the Department of Homeland Security.
(21) International adviser Richard Tanson at the University of Virginia says even minor violations stay on a student's permanent immigration record.

a|true|high18692.txt.passage:
(1) Americans often say that there are only two things a person can be sure of:death and taxes.
(2) Many people feel that the United States has the worst taxes in the world.
(3) Taxes are the money that people pay to support their government.
(4) There are generally three levels of government in the United States:federal,state,and city;therefore,there are three types of taxes.
(5) Salaried people who can earn more than four to five thousand dollars per year must pay a certain part.
(6) It depends on their salaries.
(7) The federal government has a two-level income tax:that is,15 or 28 percent.
(8) $17 850 is the cut off.
(9) The tax rate is 15 percent below $17 850 and 28 percent above.
(10) The second tax is for the state government:New York,California,or any of the other forty-eight states.
(11) Some states have an income tax similar to that of the federal government.
(12) Of course,the percentage for the state tax is lower.
(13) Other states have a sales tax,which is a percentage charged to any item which people buy in the state.
(14) Some states use income tax and sales tax to raise their revenues(=annual income).
(15) The third tax is for the city.
(16) This tax comes in two forms:property tax(people who own a home have to pay taxes on it) and excise tax ,which is collected on vehicles in a city.
(17) The cities use this money for education,police,public works,etc.
(18) Since Americans pay such high taxes,they often feel that they are working one day each week just to pay their taxes.
(19) People always complain about taxes.
(20) They often say that the government misuses their tax dollars.
(21) They all believe that taxes are too high in this country.
a|true|high18692.txt.statement: Different levels of the governments have different types of taxes in the United States.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement
   - Content words: "Different levels," "governments," "have," "different types," "taxes," "United States"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage
   - Sentence (4): "There are generally three levels of government in the United States: federal, state, and city; therefore, there are three types of taxes."
   - Sentence (7): "The federal government has a two-level income tax: that is, 15 or 28 percent."
   - Sentence (10): "The second tax is for the state government: New York, California, or any of the other forty-eight states."
   - Sentence (13): "Other states have a sales tax, which is a percentage charged to any item which people buy in the state."
   - Sentence (15): "The third tax is for the city."
   - Sentence (16): "This tax comes in two forms: property tax and excise tax, which is collected on vehicles in a city."

3. Compare content words and expressions
   - The statement summarizes the information that different levels of government (federal, state, and city) have different types of taxes.
   - The passage explicitly mentions three levels of government and describes the types of taxes associated with each level.
   - The statement rephrases this information into a more general form.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - The statement requires combining information from multiple sentences to understand that different levels of government have different types of taxes.
   - This involves a level of abstraction and generalization not directly expressed in any single sentence.
   - Therefore, it involves inference.

Answer: Inference

a|true|high17707.txt.passage:
(1) When someone meets a stranger with the same hobby, that person immediately becomes a friend.
(2) People enjoy the company of others who share a similar interest.
(3) Often they will form a club and travel great distances to attend meetings where they can talk about their hobbies.
(4) Other people have such unusual hobbies that no one else can share them.
(5) Sometimes they try to do something better, faster or longer than anyone else in order to have their names included in the Guinness Book of World Records.
(6) A lady in Massachusetts may have the world's easiest hobby; she let her hair grow.
(7) Diane Witt has not cut her hair for twelve years.
(8) It is now eight feet (2.44 meters) long, and she will soon set a new world record and her name will be placed in the Guinness Book.
(9) A thirty-year-old man in Pennsylvania played the piano for forty-five days and eleven hours, while a musician in Connecticut played the flute for forty-three hours.
(10) A teacher in California typed for over 162 hours, and a lady from Iowa spend six years typing the numbers from one to one million.
(11) Probably the cleanest people in the world are two young women from England who stayed in the shower for over ninety-eight hours; but a man in Oregon was more than twice as clean after taking a shower that lasted two hundred hours.
(12) Someone said that "records are made to be broken".
(13) One record that many young men might enjoy breaking is the one made by Roger Guy English of California.
(14) During a period of eight hours Roger kissed three thousand young women.

a|true|high22007.txt.passage:
(1) About 10 years ago, I started a job as a trainer for a telemarketing agency.
(2) In one of my first classes, the head trainer told a very inspiring story.
(3) He began by drawing a stickman standing in the middle of a circle.
(4) To make it more interesting, he drew things like a house, a car, and a few friends inside the circle.
(5) He asked the question, "Can anyone tell me what this is?"
(6) In a long silence, one guy decided to throw out "the world?"
(7) The trainer said, "That's close and this is your Comfort Zone.
(8) Inside your circle you have all the things that are important to you. "
(9) "Your home, your family, your friends, and your job.
(10) People feel that inside this circle they are safe from any danger or conflict. "
(11) "Can anyone tell me what happens when you step out of this circle?"
(12) A strong silence came over the room.
(13) The same eager guy abruptly announced, "You are afraid."
(14) Another guy said, "You make mistakes."
(15) The silence continued and the trainer smiled and said, "When you make mistakes, what can the result be?"
(16) The first guy shouted, "You learn something."
(17) "Exactly, you are learning."
(18) The trainer turned to the board and drew an arrow pointing from the stickman directly to the outside of the circle.
(19) He continued to say, "When you leave your Comfort Zone you put yourself out there, in front of the world to be in situations that you are not comfortable with."
(20) "The end result is that you have learned something that you did not know and you expand your knowledge to become a better person."
(21) He turned again to the board and drew a bigger circle around the original circle, and added a few new things like more friends, a bigger house, etc.
(22) "The moral of the story is that if you stay inside your Comfort Zone, you will never be able to expand your horizons and learn.
(23) When you step out of your Comfort Zone you will eventually make your circle bigger and your mind grow stronger, and all in all a better person."
a|true|high22007.txt.statement: If we move beyond the Comfort Zone, our anxiety level will go up.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement
   - Content words: "move beyond," "Comfort Zone," "anxiety level," "go up"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage
   - Sentence (13): "You are afraid."
   - Sentence (14): "You make mistakes."

3. Compare content words and expressions
   - The statement mentions "anxiety level," which is not explicitly stated in the passage.
   - The passage mentions "afraid," which can be interpreted as an increase in anxiety, but it is not explicitly stated that the anxiety level goes up.
   - The passage does not directly state that moving beyond the Comfort Zone will increase anxiety levels.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - The statement requires understanding that being "afraid" implies an increase in anxiety level, which is not explicitly stated.
   - The passage implies that stepping out of the Comfort Zone involves fear and potential mistakes, but it does not directly state that anxiety levels go up.
   - Therefore, the statement involves inference.

Answer: Inference

a|true|high23639.txt.passage:
(1) Lindsay Morgan Lohan was born on July 2, 1986.
(2) She was such a natural beauty that she began modeling at the age of 3, and because of her natural talent, she played parts in over 60 TV commercials for companies such as Abercrombie & Fitch, Wendy's, the Gap, Pizza Hut, Calvin Klein, and Jell-O, in which she shared the screen with Bill Cosby.
(3) When she was 7, Lohan was required to dress up as garbage for a Halloween short play on the Late Show with David Letterman called "Things You Find on the Bottom of the Subway."
(4) It was her first real acting job.
(5) In 1996, Lohan got her second big break when she was given a role on the long-running NBC soap opera "Another World."
(6) For only one season, she became the third actress to act the part Alexandra Alli Fowler.
(7) Shortly after, she hit the highway to fame when Disney sought to remake the hugely successful family comedy "The Parent Trap" (1998).
(8) In addition, she also got a three-picture deal with Disney thanks to this movie.
(9) Her next move was to appear in the Wonderful World of Disney production of "Life-Size" (2000), a comedy also featuring supermodel Tyra Banks.
(10) In 2003, along with Jamie Lee Curtis, Lindsay Lohan performed in Freaky Friday and played one of the early roles that made Jodie Foster a household name.
(11) In September 2002, Lohan also got a contract with music heavyweight Emilio Estefan Jr who is of great influence in music.
(12) She signed a five-album deal with Estefan Enterprises.
(13) In 2005, she released her first album, "Speak," followed by "A Little More Personal" the next year.

(6) Now, a new San Francisco Web-based company has taken it a step further and developed the first "brain training program" designed to actually help people improve and regain their mental sharpness.
(7) Called Lumosity, it was designed by some of the leading experts from Stanford University.
(8) Lumosity is far more than an online place to exercise your mental skills.
(9) That's because they have combined these exercises with a Web-based program that allows you to systematically improve your memory and attention skills.
(10) Apparently it works.
(11) One study showed students improved their scores on math tests by 34 percent after using Lumosity for six weeks, greater gains than those made by other students in the same class, who were not training with the Lumosity program.
(12) The company says its users have reported clearer and quicker thinking, improved memory for names, numbers, directions, increased alertness  and awareness, high mood, and better concentration at work or while driving.

(1) Advertisers tend to think big and perhaps this is why they're always getting criticism.

(13) Advertisements introduce us to new products or remind us of the existence of ones we already know about.
(14) Supposing you wanted to buy a washing machine, it is more than likely you would obtain details regarding performance, price, etc., from an advertisement.
(15) Lots of people pretend that they never read advertisements, but this claim may be seriously doubted.
(16) It is hardly possible not to read advertisements these days.
(17) And what fun they often are, too!

(19) Would you enjoy gazing at a blank wall or reading railway byelaws  while waiting for a train?
(20) Would you like to read only closely printed columns of news in your daily paper?

(32) It's the best advertisement for advertising there is!

(1) Medicine comes in lots of different packages.
(2) Painkillers in a tablet can make your headache go away.
(3) Antibiotic cream   from a tube can prevent your cuts from becoming infected.
(4) But can medicine come packaged in chicken eggs?
(5) A team of scientists from Scotland say "yes".
(6) They've engineered special chickens that lay eggs with disease-treating drugs inside.
(7) These eggs come from chickens that have been engineered to produce certain drugs inside their egg whites.
(8) These drugs are made of molecules called proteins .
(9) Animals make thousands of proteins -- they're the main element in skin, hair, milk and meat.
(10) Since animals can make proteins easily, they're good candidates   for making protein drugs.
(11) Researchers have already made cows, sheep and goats produce protein drugs in their milk.
(12) But chickens are cheaper to take care of, need less room, and grow faster than these other animals.
(13) Those qualities could make chickens a better choice to become living drug factories, says Simon Lillico of the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh, Scotland.
(14) Lillico and a team of researchers changed chickens' DNA -- the code that tells cells how to make proteins -- so that the birds' cells made two protein drugs.
(15) One drug can treat skin cancer, and the other treats a nerve disease called multiple sclerosis  .
(16) The scientists changed the chickens' DNA so that the birds made these drugs only in their egg whites.
(17) This protects the chickens' bodies from the drugs' possible harmful effects and makes it easy for scientists to collect the drugs.

d0|notTrue|high23123.txt.passage:
(1) NOT all math skills are learned in the classroom.
(2) Some of them come naturally.
(3) Consider the split-second calculations  you make when you estimate   the number of empty seats on the school bus or the number of cookies in a cookie jar.
(4) These estimates can often be done without counting.
(5) That's because humans are born with the ability to closely guess the number of items in a group.
(6) Researchers call this ability a person's "number sense".
(7) To see how a person's inborn number sense might be linked to mathematical performance in the classroom, psychologist Justin Halberda and his colleagues from Johns Hopkins University in prefix = st1 /Baltimore,USran some tests.
(8) The scientists asked 64 14-year-olds to look at images   of yellow and blue dots that flashed on a computer screen for a fraction   of a second.
(9) Each image contained between 10 and 32 dots that varied in size.
(10) Some images contained twice as many blue dots  as yellow dots.
(11) In other images, however, the number of blue and yellow dots was nearly equal.
(12) For each image, the students were asked to estimate which color1 had more dots.
(13) The scientists found a wide variation in how well students could pick the color1 with the most dots.
(14) The scientists then looked at the students' math scores dating back to kindergarten.
(15) Children that performed best in the image test also scored the highest in standard math tests.
(16) The same finding held true at the other end of the spectrum  .
(17) Students who didn't score well on the image test received lower math scores.
(18) The study was the first to show a link between a person's inborn number sense and his or her achievement in formal math training.
(19) Does this connection mean that one cannot be good in math if they have a weak number sense?
(20) Or that having a strong number sense is a guarantee for good grades in math?
(21) The answers are not clear.
(22) While scientists continue looking at the possible links between a person's number sense and math achievement, one thing is certain: Doing lots of math homework will increase your chances of success.

(12) Simona's parents were able to ease the tension in the family.
(13) Her mother also now attends parenting classes and support groups at the Center.
(14) The Community Resource Center provides not only many needed resources, but also a place where women can gain skills and self-confidence.
(15) It provides children with a place where they are accepted.
(16) At the Center parents and children find a place where they can grow and find the support they need to stay together and care for each other.

a|notTrue|high10071.txt.passage:
(1) I took the bus to work for many years.
(2) No one knew each other; the passengers all sat there sleepily in the morning.
(3) The bus was cheerless and silent.
(4) One of the passengers was a small gray-haired man who took the bus to the center for senior citizens every morning.
(5) No one ever paid very much attention to him.
(6) One July morning he said good morning to the driver and smiled.
(7) The driver nodded guardedly .
(8) The rest of us were silent.
(9) The next day, the old man boarded with a big smile and said in a loud voice: "A very good morning to you all!"
(10) Some of us looked up, amazed, and murmured "Good Morning" in reply.
(11) The following weeks, our friend was dressed in a nice old suit and tie.
(12) His thin hair had been carefully combed.
(13) He said good morning to us every day and we gradually began to nod and talk to each other.
(14) One morning he even had a bunch of wild flowers in his hand.
(15) The driver turned around smiling and asked: "Have you got yourself a girlfriend, Charlie?"
(16) We never got to know if his name really was "Charlie", but he nodded shyly and said yes.
(17) The other passengers whistled and clapped at him.
(18) Charlie waved the flowers before he sat down on his seat.
(19) Every morning after that Charlie always brought flowers.
(20) Some passengers also brought flowers for him.
(21) The bus became a happy place.
(22) Then, one morning, as autumn was closing in, Charlie wasn't waiting at his usual stop.
(23) When he wasn't there the next day and the day after that, we started wondering if he was sick or--hopefully - on holiday somewhere.
(24) When we came nearer to the center for senior citizens, one of the passengers asked the driver to wait.
(25) We all held our breaths when he went to the door.
(26) The old gentle man was fine, but one of his close friends had died over the weekend.
(27) How silent we were the rest of the way to work.
(28) The next Monday Charlie was waiting at the stop, stooping   a bit more, a little bit more gray, and without a tie.
(29) Inside the bus was silent.
(30) Even though no one had talked about it, all of us sat there silently, our eyes filled with tears and a bunch of wild flowers in our hands.

(1) Are you a big fan of pirate  stories?
(2) Do you know how to talk like a pirate?
(3) You can have fun on September 19th.
(4) This special holiday is called International Talk Like A Pirate Day.
(5) It was invented in 1995 by two young Americans, John Baur and Mark Summers.
(6) These two good friends came up with this idea when they were playing racquetball, and began to talk like pirates.
(7) At that moment, they thought, "Young people are interested in pirate, but there is no such a chance for people to get together to share it.
(8) Why not have a day when everybody talks like a pirate?"
(9) They decided to make September 19th the date.
(10) Since then, Baur and Summers have tried hard to promote Talk Like A Pirate Day.
(11) As Baur said, "We've talked like pirates, and encouraged several of our friends to do so every September 19th."
(12) At first, this day was only known by a few people.
(13) In 2002, Baur and Summers sent a letter about their invented holiday to Dave Barry.
(14) This columnist really liked their idea.
(15) He promoted the holiday and made it known to more and more people.
(16) Now, it has really become an international holiday.
(17) On Talk Like A Pirate Day, you need to dress like a pirate.
(18) You should wear a big hat or maybe cover one of your eyes.
(19) You can pretend to be any kind of pirate that you can find in any story or movie.
(20) Having fun and being relaxed are not the only best things about the holiday.
(21) Young people share the same interest and childhood dreams that day.
(22) That's why it is welcomed by so many people, especially young people.
(23) Unlike traditional holidays, International Talk Like A Pirate Day is a brand new holiday which is invented by the youth themselves and celebrated by themselves too.
(24) Because of Baur and Summers' imagination and creativity, thousands of young people's pirate dream come true.
(25) Next time, when you have a good idea, why not share it with your fiends and insist on your belief?
(26) Maybe you can create another wonderful holiday for the youth in the future.

a|notTrue|high20138.txt.passage:
(1) How To Apply for USA Passports Online
(2) If you live in the United States, and you're planning a trip abroad, it is important to apply for new passports at least 2 months in advance (to avoid extra charges for an expedited passport processing).
(3) You can now get the application for a passport online.
(4) Some directions are detailed below:
(5) Downloading the passport application: The USA passport application, Form DS-11, is available for download on the US Department of State's website.
(6) *The passport application form is a Portable Document Format (PDF) file, and your computer must have Adobe Acrobat Reader to open it.
(7) If you do not have Adobe Acrobat Reader, you must first download the Adobe Acrobat software.
(8) You can do so by clicking here.
(9) *If you would like to download a blank application,      click here.
(10) If you would like to fill out the application online, then print it,click here.
(11) Printing the passport application: When printing the passport application, there are several requirements that must be followed.
(12) *You must use standard paper that is sized 8 1/2 inches by 11 inches.
(13) *The printing must be clear and legible.
(14) *The paper must be a standard medium weight, matte white (not shiny), and ink must be black.
(15) *The printer's ink must be black and there may not be any holes or perforations in the paper.
(16) *After printing, make sure that the box for pasting on your passport photo is the correct size of 2 inches by 2 inches.
(17) *It is important to print the application on one-sided paper.
(18) An application that is printed on double-sided paper will not be accepted.
(19) *For more information on these guidelines, visit the US Department of State's website.
(20) Filling out the passport application: As stated above, there are two ways to fill out the passport application: 1) typing your answers into the online form 2) writing answers by hand, in black ink, or by use of a typewriter.
(21) In both cases, you will need to provide the following information.
(22) *Your full name
(23) *Age
(24) *Date of birth
(25) *Place of birth
(26) *Phone number
(27) *Hair color1
(28) *Social security number
(29) *Mailing address 
(30) *Permanent Address.
(31) *Occupation
(32) *Employer
(33) *Your parents' names and birth information
(34) *Your spouse's name and birth information
(35) *Any other names you have used previously
(36) *Your travel plans
(37) *Emergency contact information
(38) Signing the application: It is important that you do not sign the application until you take the oath administered only by an authorized passport acceptance agent.
(39) Submitting the passport application: Along with the completed passport application, you must submit the following:
(40) *Proof of US Citizenship
(41) *Proof of Identity
(42) *Two recent passport sized (2x2 inches) photographs
(43) *The passport application fee

inferenceDetection:

4. Determine whether inference is required

(1) The sunflower plant bears bright yellow flowers that always face the sun, hence the name.
(2) However, the plant is grown not only for its beautiful flowers, but also for its oil that has a host of health benefits.
(3) Sunflower oil prevents diseases, lowers cholesterol levels, keeps the heart healthy, and even prevents certain forms of cancer.
(4) Sunflower oil is a kind of "magic oil" when it comes to skin care, as it makes your skin soft and smooth, and prevents signs of aging.
(5) So, let us know more about its effects on your skin.
(6) 1. It Acts as an Effective Emollient .
(7) Sunflower oil is an effective emollient, which means that it helps the skin retain moisture.
(8) Thus, people with dry skin can benefit immensely from the regular use of the oil.
(9) Also, it is mild, and provides relief from irritation and inflammation of the skin.
(10) 2. It Offers Sun Protection.
(11) Sunflower oil is a rich source of vitamin E. The high vitamin content prevents the sensitive cells of the skin from being damaged by the ultraviolet (UV) rays of the sun.
(12) Also, the beta-carotene (b-) present in sunflower oil helps neutralize  free radicals that are responsible for causing damage to the skin, including sunburn and skin cancers.
(13) 3. It Prevents Earlier Aging.

(15) What's more, regular use of sunflower oil can reduce wrinkles and make one look younger.
(16) Also, sunflower oil is rich in antioxidants  that keep the skin fresh.
(17) 4. It Prevents Skin Infections .

a|notTrue|high1431.txt.passage:
(1) Nothing is more frustrating than not being able to sleep, tossing and turning  .
(2) Your mind is racing, going over everything that happened that day.
(3) Night noises keep you awake.
(4) What can you do?
(5) Here are some things you can do to help you to fall asleep.
(6) Sleep only when sleepy
(7) This reduces the time you are awake in bed.
(8) If you can't fall asleep within 20 minutes, get up and do something boring until you feel sleepy.
(9) Don't expose yourself to bright light while you are up.
(10) The light tells your brain that it is time to wake up.
(11) Don't take naps
(12) This will ensure that you are tired at bedtime.
(13) If you just can't make it through the day without a nap, sleep less than one hour, and take a nap before 3 p.m.
(14) Get up and go to bed at the same time every day
(15) Even on weekends!
(16) When your sleep cycle has a regular rhythm, you will feel better.
(17) Regular exercise can help you sleep well, but the time to do exercise is important.
(18) Exercising in the morning or early afternoon will not interfere   with sleep.
(19) Have a snack before bedtime
(20) If your stomach is too empty, that can interfere with sleep.
(21) However, if you eat a heavy meal before bedtime, that can interfere as well.
(22) Have a warm glass of milk.
(23) Take a hot bath 90 minutes before bedtime
(24) A hot bath will raise your body temperature, but it is the drop in body temperature that may make you feel sleepy.

(1) If you're among the millions of Americans packing their vacation bags for summer trips,don't forget,however, that as soon as you step off the plane,you're expected to follow that country's laws.
(2) We took a look at one of the top countries Americans are most likely to visit and what might happen if you got caught.
(3) prefix = st1 /Mexico
(4) Smoking marijuana:If police find you smoking a cigarette which contains marijuana,they will most likely detain you overnight and you will get a fine.
(5) Carrying a firearm:You might be able to walk around with a gun in Texas,but that isn't so in Mexico,so leave your gun behind.
(6) If you wish to go hunting in Mexico.
(7) you can apply for a gun permit but expect to be charged $5,000.
(8) If you are caught bringing a firearm,Mexican policeman may take away your car and arrest you.
(9) An arrested American may spend months in prison while his or her case is being looked into.
(10) Driving 70 mph on the highway:Speed limits are on the lower side.
(11) Depending on what type of highway you're driving on,you shouldn't go faster than 62 mph.
(12) The police enforce the speed limit,but most local police don't have radars.
(13) The maximum fine for speeding is about $40.
(14) To scare tourists into paying the fine "on the spot",sometimes police will threaten to keep your driver's license.
(15) This is illegal, so call them on it.

a|notTrue|high23129.txt.passage:
(1) When she looked ahead, Florence Chadwick saw nothing but a solid wall of fog.
(2) Her body was numb .She had been swimming for nearly sixteen hours.
(3) Already she was the first woman to swim the English Channel in both directions.
(4) Now, at age 34,her goal was to become the first woman to swim from Catalina Island to the California coast.
(5) On that fourth of July morning in 1952,the sea was like an ice bath and the fog was so thick that she could hardly see her support boats.
(6) Sharks swam toward her lone figure, only to be driven away by rifle shots.
(7) Against the frigid grip of the sea, she struggled on--hour after hour--while millions watched on national television.
(8) Alongside Florence in one of the boats , her mother and her trainer offered encouragement.
(9) They told her it wasn't much farther.
(10) But all she could see was fog.
(11) They urged her not to give up.
(12) But after 15 hours, she still felt she couldn’t go on.
(13) With only a half mile to go, she asked to be pulled out.
(14) Still thawing her chilled body several hours later, she told a reporter," Look, I'm not excusing myself, but if I could have seen land I might have made it."
(15) It was not fatigue or even the cold water that defeated her .
(16) It was the fog.
(17) She was unable to see her goal.
(18) Two months later, she tried again.
(19) This time ,despite the same dense fog, she swam with her faith intact and her goal clearly pictured in her mind.
(20) She knew that somewhere behind that fog was land and this time she made it!
(21) Florence Chadwick became the first woman to swim the Catalina Channel, eclipsing the men's record by two hours!

(1) After two classes, I started to recognize several of the faces in each class.
(2) There was always someone braver than the others who would introduce themselves and ask me questions about how I was liking Forks.
(3) I tried to be , so mostly I just lied a lot to appear to be skilled at dealing with people.
(4) At least I never needed the map.
(5) One girl sat next to me in both Trig and Spanish, and she walked with me to the cafeteria for lunch.
(6) She was tiny, several inches shorter than my five feet four inches, but her wildly curly dark hair made up a lot of the difference between our heights.
(7) I couldn't remember her name, so I smiled and nodded as she gossiped about teachers and classes.
(8) I didn't try to keep up.
(9) We sat at the end of a full table with several of her friends, who she introduced to me.
(10) I forgot all their names as soon as she spoke them.
(11) They seemed impressed by her bravery in speaking to me.
(12) The boy from England Eric, waved at me from across the room.
(13) It was there, sitting in the lunchroom, trying to make conversation with seven curious strangers, that I first saw them.
(14) They were sitting in the corner of the cafeteria, as far away from where I sat as possible in the long room.
(15) There were five of them.
(16) They weren't talking, and they weren't eating, though they each had a tray of untouched food in front of them.
(17) They weren't staring at me, unlike most of the other students, so it was safe to stare at them without fear of meeting an over interested pair of eyes.
(18) But it was none of these things that caught and held my attention.
(19) I stared because their faces, so different, so similar, were all extremely, inhumanly beautiful.
(20) They were faces you never expected to see except perhaps on the airbrushed pages of a fashion magazine, or painted by an old master as the face of an angel.
(21) It was hard to decide who was the most beautiful -- maybe the perfect blond girl, or the bronze-haired boy.

(1) Every language has its own special words and expressions.
(2) And a story can be told about each of them.

(14) Another expression is "not so hot".
(15) If you ask someone how she feels, she may answer: "Not so hot. "
(16) What she means is that she does not feel well.
(17) "Not so hot" is also a way of saying that you do not really like something.
(18) "A hot shot" is a person, often a young person, who thinks he can do anything.
(19) At least he wants to try.
(20) He is very sure he can succeed.
(21) But often he fails.
(22) The expression came from the army.
(23) A hot shot was a soldier who fired without aiming carefully.

(25) An angry person's neck often becomes red.

(28) But he got hot under the collar when someone took his radio.
(29) The hot line was a direct communication link between the leaders of the Soviet Union  and the United States.
(30) The hot line had an important purpose: to prevent accidental war between the two competitors during the period known as the Cold War.
(31) The American president and the Soviet leader were able to communicate directly and immediately on the hot line.

(1) Every language has its own special words and expressions.
(2) And a story can be told about each of them.
(3) "Hot" is a simple, easily-understood word.
(4) So are most of the expressions made with the word "hot".
(5) But not always, as we shall see.
(6) Take the phrase "hot potato" for example.
(7) The potato is a popular vegetable in the United States.
(8) Many people like baked potatoes, cooked in an oven or fire.
(9) Imagine trying to carry a hot, baked potato in your hand.
(10) It would be difficult, even painful, to do so.
(11) One such hot potato is taxes.
(12) Calling for higher taxes can mean defeat for a politician.
(13) And yet, if taxes are not raised, some very popular government programs could be cut, which can also make a politician very unpopular.
(14) Another expression is "not so hot".
(15) If you ask someone how she feels, she may answer: "Not so hot. "
(16) What she means is that she does not feel well.
(17) "Not so hot" is also a way of saying that you do not really like something.
(18) "A hot shot" is a person, often a young person, who thinks he can do anything.
(19) At least he wants to try.
(20) He is very sure he can succeed.
(21) But often he fails.
(22) The expression came from the army.
(23) A hot shot was a soldier who fired without aiming carefully.
(24) A person who becomes angry easily is called "a hothead".
(25) An angry person's neck often becomes red.
(26) We say he is "hot under the collar".
(27) You could say that your friend is no hothead.
(28) But he got hot under the collar when someone took his radio.
(29) The hot line was a direct communication link between the leaders of the Soviet Union  and the United States.
(30) The hot line had an important purpose: to prevent accidental war between the two competitors during the period known as the Cold War.
(31) The American president and the Soviet leader were able to communicate directly and immediately on the hot line.

(1) As the new semester begins, millions of college students across the country are trying hard to remember how best to write a paper or, more likely, how best to delay that paper.
(2) Procrastination is the thief of time and a lot of students suffer from it.
(3) They can spend whole days in the library doing nothing but staring into space, eating snacks, surfing the Internet, watching videos and looking at other students sitting around them, who, most likely, are doing nothing either.
(4) Paralyzed   by their habit to procrastinate, they write micro blogs about their fears, asking their online friends if they sometimes have the same issue.
(5) But this does nothing to break the spell  .
(6) According to a recent report, 95 percent of us procrastinate at some point and 20 percent of the world's population are always procrastinating.
(7) The figures are disappointing.
(8) Procrastinators are less wealthy, less healthy and less happy than those who don't delay.
(9) Procrastinators like to find excuses to justify their behavior, but BBC columnist Rowan Pelling says they are all wrong.
(10) Many procrastinators tell themselves they are perfectionists who work best under pressure.
(11) Pelling says this is nonsense, as work done at the last minute is more likely to have mistakes than work done on time.
(12) The behavior of procrastinators often makes them feel ashamed, inconveniences others and annoys loved ones.
(13) Pelling also points out that procrastination feels particularly delinquent  in a society that thinks of speedy action as admirable, and, at times, even as a moral good.
(14) Fortunately, social scientists have thrown their weight behind efforts to understand this behavioral mistake and offer strategies to control it.
(15) Piers Steel, a Canadian social scientist and author of The Procrastination Equation, believes human is "designed" to procrastinate.
(16) Nevertheless, he suggests a couple of good ways to get through the task at hand.

a|notTrue|high20329.txt.passage:
(1) Once upon a time two brothers who lived on neighbouring farms fell into conflict.
(2) It was the first serious one in 40 years of farming side by side, sharing machinery, and trading labor and goods as needed .
(3) Then the long cooperoration fell apart.
(4) It began with a small misunderstanding and it grew into a major difference, and finally it exploded into an exchange of bitter words followed by weeks of silence.
(5) One morning there was a knock on John's door.
(6) He opened it to find a man with a carpenter's toolbox.
(7) "I'm looking for a few days' work" he said.
(8) "Perhaps you would have a few small jobs here and there I could help with.
(9) Could I help you?"
(10) "Yes," said the older brother.
(11) "I do have a job for you.
(12) Look across the creek  at that farm.
(13) That's my neighbor, in fact, it's my younger brother.
(14) Last week there was a meadow  between us and he took his bulldozer  to the river levee  and now there is a creek between us.
(15) Well, he may have done this to spite me, but I'll go him one better.
(16) I want you to build me a fence -- an 8-foot fence -- so I won't need to see his place or his face any more.
(17) Cool him down , anyhow."
(18) The carpenter said, "I think I understand the situation.
(19) I'll be able to do a job that pleases you."
(20) The older brother had to go to town, so he helped the carpenter get the materials ready and then he was off for the day.
(21) The carpenter worked hard all that day measuring, sawing, nailing, and hammering.
(22) About sunset when the farmer returned, the carpenter had just finished his job.
(23) The farmer's eyes opened wide, his jaw dropped.
(24) There was no fence there at all.
(25) It was a bridge -- a bridge stretching from one side of the creek to the other!
(26) A fine piece of work -- and his younger brother, was coming across, his hand spread out ..
(27) "You are quite a fellow to build this bridge after all I've said and done."
(28) The two brothers stood at each end of the bridge, and then they met in the middle, taking each other's hand.
(29) They turned to see the carpenter lift his toolbox on his shoulder.
(30) "No, wait!
(31) Stay a few days.
(32) I've a lot of other projects for you," said the older brother.
(33) "I'd love to stay on," the carpenter said, "but, I have many more bridges to build."

(8) Each month another new diet appears claiming to be the diet to end all diets.
(9) If diets really worked, then why are there so many of them?
(10) The reason a new diet pops up each month, is that last's month's diets did not work.
(11) The truth of the matter is that DIETS DON'T WORK.
(12) The diet and fashion industries are not totally to blame for society's obsession with thinness.
(13) We are the ones keeping them in business.
(14) We buy into the idea that we can attain the "ideal" body image.
(15) We allow ourselves to believe the lies being thrown at us constantly.
(16) We are throwing away our hard earned money trying to live up to the standards that society has set for us.
(17) It's unfortunate, but in today's society, people have forgotten that it's what's inside a person that counts, not what's on the outside.
(18) We need to start loving and accepting each other for who we are, not what we look like.
(19) Once again, I would like to stress the fact that diets don't work.
(20) Eating three healthy meals a day, a few snacks and doing moderate exercise, will allow your body to go to its natural set point.
(21) Next time you decide that you are going to start another diet because you feel you are too fat, stop, sign up for a self-esteem class instead.

a|true|college528.txt.passage:
(1) Sloth may be seen as a sin, but some of history's most accomplished men were fond of lounging around.
(2) Leonardo da Vinci enjoyed napping.
(3) So did Albert Einstein and Winston Churchill.
(4) Richard Buckminster Fuller advocated taking 30-minute naps every six hours.
(5) He is reported to have abandoned the practice only because" his schedule conflicted with that of his business associates, who insisted on sleeping like other men."
(6) No one has yet proved a correlation between napping and artistic brilliance or professional success, but an intriguing study published this week in the Archives of Internal Medicine claims to find a link between daytime siestas and good health.
(7) A team of researchers led by Androniki Naska of the University of Athens Medical School and Dimitrios Trichopoulos of Harvard's School of Public Health followed over 23,000 Greek patients with no history of coronary disease, cancer or stroke, for an average of six years.
(8) Their conclusion: napping just might save your life.
(9) The study found that the group of adults who took siestas (defined as 30-minute naps) at least three times a week had a third fewer deaths from heart disease than an equivalent group who did not sleep at all during the day.
(10) The benefit was greater for men than for women.
(11) (Whether women benefited at all was hard to estimate as there were too few deaths among them during the course of the study.)
(12) It was also greater for working men than for those who had retired.
(13) However, a number of previous studies done in the Mediterranean and in parts of Central America (where siestas remain common) have come up with conflicting results, but Dr Naska and Dr Trichopoulos argue that those studies have often been flawed.
(14) The subjects in some, for example, had survived heart attacks and may therefore have benefited more from napping than healthy individuals do.
(15) Given that all of the subjects of this new study were Greek, could the much-celebrated Mediterranean diet deserve credit, rather than the siestas?
(16) The firm answer from Dr Trichopoulos is" No" .
(17) And he is in a good position to say so, for it was he who did the pioneering research that put olive oil and a plant-based diet on the scientific map in the first place.
(18) Unlike some other siesta studies, his was controlled for diet, smoking, exercise and other relevant variables.
(19) The earlier findings about the benefits of the Mediterranean diet are confirmed, he says, and napping seems to help on top of that.
(20) Before buying a sofa for the office, however, it might be wise to consider the possibility of selection bias.
(21) Dr Trichopoulos concedes that" Type A"  personalities, whose hard-working lives may make them prone to heart attacks, are also much less likely to take naps during the day.
(22) That bias might be skewing the study's results.
(23) Even so, he advises, " Take a nap if you can."

a|true|college990.txt.passage:
(1) My father was a foreman of a sugar-cane plantation in Rio Piedras, Puerto Rico.
(2) My first job was to drive the oxen that ploughed the cane fields.
(3) I would walk behind an ox, guiding him with a broomstick.
(4) For $ 1 a day, I worked eight hours straight, with no food breaks.
(5) It was very tedious work, but it prepared me for life and taught me many lasting lessons.
(6) Because the plantation owners were always watching us, I had to be on time every day and work as hard as I could.
(7) I've never been late for any job since.
(8) I also learned about being respectful and faithful to the people you work for.
(9) More important, I earned my pay; it never entered my mind to say I was sick just because I didn't want to work.
(10) I was only six years old, but I was doing a man's job.
(11) Our family needed every dollar we could make because my father never earned more than $ 18 a week.
(12) Our home was a three-room wood shack with a dirty floor and no toilet.
(13) Nothing made me prouder than bringing home money to help my mother, father, two brothers and three sisters.
(14) This gave me self-esteem, one of the most important things a person can have.
(15) When I was seven, I got work at a golf course near our house.
(16) My job was to stand down the fairway and spot the balls as they landed, so the golfers could find them.
(17) Losing a ball meant you were fired, so I never missed one.
(18) Some nights I would lie in bed and dreamt of making thousands of dollars by playing golf and being able to buy a bicycle.
(19) The more I dreamed, the more I thought.
(20) Why not?
(21) I made my first golf club out of guava limb and a piece of pipe.
(22) Then I hammered an empty tin can into the shape of a ball.
(23) And finally I dug two small holes in the ground and hit the ball back and forth.
(24) I practiced with the same devotion and intensity.
(25) I learned working in the field - except now I was driving golf balls with club, not oxen with a broomstick.

a|true|college1126.txt.passage:
(1) A strong rupee is giving policymakers a new sort of headache
(2) THE Indian finance ministry's mid-year review, released this week, sees the external sector as a silver lining around the country's huge fiscal deficit. "
(3) Buoyant" and "encouraging" are the words used to describe three consecutive quarters of current-account surplus--the first in a quarter-century.
(4) Add to that swelling foreign-exchange reserves and a stronger rupee, and some are arguing that it is time for drastic liberalisation of India's foreign-exchange regime.
(5) They could be disappointed.
(6) For most of the past decade, the nominal value of the rupee has been allowed to decline gently against the dollar, by about 5% a year, thus staying fairly steady in real terms.
(7) This year, however, it has been appreciating in real terms (and, since June, nominally as well).
(8) It would have done so more sharply had the central bank not been buying dollars with gusto.
(9) Exporters of manufactured goods, obsessed with price competition from China, are aghast at the rise--and at the prospect held out by some forecasters that a sustained boom in India's IT exports means it will continue.
(10) The rupee's recent strength is only partly related to India's prowess in software and the mushrooming of "business-process outsourcing" in such projects as call-centres.
(11) The chunky surplus on invisibles owes more to remittances: non-resident Indians, attracted by the stability of the rupee and its higher interest rates, have been moving their offshore deposits back home.
(12) Similarly, Indian companies are borrowing more in dollars without selling rupees forward to hedge repayments.
(13) The trade deficit, meanwhile, has been shrinking, as imports grow slowly.
(14) The inflows have boosted foreign-exchange reserves by some $20 billion this year, to $66 billion, or 12 months'-worth of imports.
(15) The size of this cushion has triggered some calls for further liberalisation of the labyrinthine foreign-exchange controls that India still maintains, despite the move in 1993 towards rupee convertibility for trade purposes.
(16) In recent months, some controls have duly been eased.
(17) It is now simpler, for example, for individuals to open foreign-currency bank accounts, and for travellers to get hold of foreign exchange.
(18) And non-resident Indians have been allowed to take out money acquired through inheritance, or from rents and dividends.
(19) Some commentators have taken all this as a harbinger of full capital-account convertibility.
(20) That is not on the cards.
(21) The experience of 1991, when India ran out of money, has left the central bank prone to caution--an approach it felt was vindicated by the East Asian crisis of 1997-98.
(22) With war in Iraq looming and a turbulent oil market, some risk aversion is understandable.
(23) India's fiscal deficit--some 10% of GDP and widening--is another reason for moving slowly.
(24) Just as one rating agency, Moody's, is considering upgrading India's external debt, another, Fitch, has warned that its local-currency rating is under threat.
(25) Nor is it certain that opening the capital account would mean a weaker rupee.
(26) It might even attract more capital inflows.
(27) As India's exporters are learning, convertibility is a two-way street.

a|true|college3724.txt.passage:
(1) Most growing plants contain much more water than all other materials combined.
(2) C. R. Barnes has suggested that it is as proper to term the plant a water structure as to call a house composed mainly of brick a brick building.
(3) Certain it is that all essential processes of plant growth and development occur in water.
(4) The mineral elements from the soil that are usable by the plant must be dissolved in the soil solution before they can be taken into the root.
(5) They are carried to all parts of the growing plant and are built into essential plant materials while in a dissolved state.
(6) The carbon dioxide (CO2) from the air may enter the leaf as a gas but is dissolved in water in the leaf before it is combined with a part of the water to form simple sugars -- the base material from which the plant body is mainly built.
(7) Actively growing plant parts are generally 75 to 90 percent water.
(8) Structural parts of plants, such as woody stems no longer actively growing, may have much less water than growing tissues.
(9) The actual amount of water in the plant at any one time, however, is only a very small part of what passes through it during its development.
(10) The processes of photosynthesis, by which carbon dioxide and water are combined -- in the presence of chlorophyll  and with energy derived from light -- to form sugars, require that carbon dioxide from the air enter the plant.
(11) This occurs mainly in the leaves.
(12) The leaf surface is not solid but contains great numbers of minute openings, through which the carbon dioxide enters.
(13) The same structure that permits the one gas to enter the leaf, however, permits another gas -- water vapor -- to be lost from it.
(14) Since carbon dioxide is present in the air only in trace quantities (3 to 4 parts in 10,000 parts of air) and water vapor is near saturation in the air spaces within the leaf (at 80℉, saturated air would contain about 186 parts of water vapor in 10,000 parts of air), the total amount of water vapor lost is many times the carbon dioxide intake.
(15) Actually, because of wind and other factors, the loss of water in proportion to carbon dioxide intake may be even greater than the relative concentrations of the two gases.
(16) Also, not all of the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized into carbohydrates .

a|true|college486.txt.passage:
(1) Sometimes it seems as if Google has never come across an industry it doesn't want to disrupt.
(2) Best known for its hugely popular search engine, the Internet giant has spread its tentacles into an ever-growing array of businesses, including advertising, telecoms and, most recently, digital-navigation software.
(3) The company's habit of selling services cheaply or giving them away for free has endeared it to consumers.
(4) But its tactics have enraged competitors, who complain their new rival is out to destroy the economics of entire industries.
(5) Such griping has been loudest in the worlds of publishing and entertainment.
(6) Although media companies are hooked on the money they mint via adverts that run on Google and its YouTube video-streaming business, many of them also accuse the search firm of commoditising their content and of undermining their profits by making it easy for marketers to track the effectiveness of online ad spend. "
(7) You're fucking with the magic,"  says the boss of one big media company to Google's founders in a memorable scene recounted at the start of Ken Auletta's new book.
(8) Mr Auletta, an American journalist and long-time commentator on the media industry, dismisses claims that Google's programming wizards are to blame for putting a jinx on the media world.
(9) Instead, he places the blame squarely, and correctly, on the publishing and movie executives who failed to appreciate the speed with which the Internet would sap their companies' fortunes.
(10) They were also slow to spot that, although Google presented itself as a friend, it had all the hallmarks of a powerful enemy too.
(11) Now the frenemy has become a scapegoat for many of the industry's self-inflicted wounds.
(12) Mr Auletta does a respectable job of reviewing the media companies' predicaments.
(13) He also rehearses some of the well-known elements of Google's culture that have helped transform it from a start-up launched in a garage 11 years ago to a colossus with ambitions to become the world's first media company with revenues of $100 billion.
(14) Among other things, these include lavish stock options, perks such as free meals and massages, and a rigorous and sometimes quirky recruitment process.
(15) More compelling are the book's insights into the relationship between the members of the triumvirate that runs Google-Mr Brin, Larry Page, his co-founder, and Eric Schmidt, the chief executive, who arrived at Google several years after its launch.
(16) Brought in at the behest of venture capitalists that have backed the firm, Mr Schmidt found himself treading a delicate path between the sensibilities of the brilliant but socially awkward founders and the demands of the impatient financiers.
(17) With the help of a veteran Silicon Valley executive who acts as a coach, the three men ultimately developed an effective working relationship.
(18) Which is just as well, for Google now faces some formidable challenges.
(19) Fast-growing social networks such as Facebook are after a much bigger chunk of online ad dollars.
(20) And Google's size has begun to attract the attention of anti-trust watchdogs in areas such as digital book scanning, where it has ambitious plans.
(21) This hardly amounts to the end of Google's dominance as we know it.
(22) But if the company misplays its hand, it could turn out to be the beginning of the end.

a|true|college585.txt.passage:
(1) Neither Oliver Williamson of the University of California at Berkeley nor Elinor Ostrom of Indiana University at Bloomington was widely tipped to win this year's Nobel Prize for economics.
(2) This may be because their work sits at the boundary of economics, law and political science, and tackles different questions to the ones that economists have traditionally studied.
(3) Mr. Williamson and Ms. Ostrom work independently of each other but both have contributed plenty to economists' understanding of which institutions-firms, markets, governments, or informal systems of social norms, for example-are best suited for conducting different types of economic transactions.
(4) Ronald Coase, a British economist who won the Nobel Prize in 1991, argued that in some situations, and for some kinds of transactions, administrative decision-making within a single legal entity is more efficient than a straightforward market transaction.
(5) Mr. Coase's arguments were influential and convinced economists that the internal workings of organisations were worth paying attention to explicitly.
(6) But it was left to Mr. Williamson to refine Mr. Coase's theory and clarify what features of certain transactions made carrying them out more efficient within a firm rather than in the market.
(7) Mr. Williamson showed that complex transactions involving investment decisions that are much more valuable within a relationship than to a third party are best done within a firm.
(8) Part of the problem, he argued, was that some economic transactions are so complicated, and involve so many things which could go wrong, that writing a legally enforceable contract that takes all possibilities into account is impossible.
(9) Simpler transactions are completed easily in markets; more complicated ones may demand firms.
(10) But in later work he also showed that organising matters within companies had costs: in particular, it relied on internal authority to get things done, and this could be abused.
(11) Ms. Ostrom has concentrated on a different aspect of economic governance.
(12) She has spent her life studying how human societies manage common resources such as forests, rivers, pastures or wildlife.
(13) Just as with public goods, it is difficult to prevent people from using the commons.
(14) But unlike public goods, and like private ones, what one person takes leaves less for others.
(15) Economic theory then predicts that rational individuals will overuse these resources.
(16) Economists have tended to emphasise property rights as a solution to the problem of managing common resources.
(17) But Ms. Ostrom spent much of her early career studying how communities managed such common resources.
(18) She found that groups of people tended to have complex sets of rules, norms and penalties to ensure that such resources were used sustainably.
(19) Such self-governance often worked well.
(20) Successful informal institutions, she found, have certain features in common, which sets them apart from institutions that fail.
(21) The principles of game theory, particularly the theory of repeated interactions, proved remarkably useful in formulating general principles of how common resources ought to be managed without necessarily resorting to private or state ownership.
(22) Mr Williamson launched an entire branch of economic theorising which looks more deeply into firms than economists had tended to do previously.
(23) His theories have also helped with understanding the choice between equity and debt, and corporate finance more generally.
(24) Ms. Ostrom's research has spawned many experiments about how people interact strategically.
(25) Some of these have influenced game theory, which originally provided Ms. Ostrom with her analytical tools.

a|true|college3016.txt.passage:
(1) The biographer has to dance between two shaky positions with respect to the subject.
(2) Too close a relation, and the writer may be objectivity.
(3) Not close enough, and the writer may lack the sympathy necessary to any effort to portray a mind, a soul-the quality of life.
(4) Who should write the biography of a family, for example?
(5) Because of their closeness to the subject, family members may have special information, but by the same token, they may not have the distance that would allow them to be fair.
(6) Similarly, a king's servant might not be the best one to write a biography of that king.
(7) But a foreigner might not have the knowledge and sympathy necessary to write the king's biography-not for a readership from within the kingdom, at any rate.
(8) There is no ideal position for such a task.
(9) The biographer has to work with the position he or she has in the world, adjusting that position as necessary to deal with the subject.
(10) Every position has strengths and weaknesses: to thrive, a writer must try to become aware of these, evaluate them in terms of the subject, and select a position accordingly.
(11) When their subjects are heroes or famous figures, biographies often reveal a democratic motive: they attempt to show that their subjects are only human, no better than anyone else.
(12) Other biographies are meant to change us, to invite us to become better than we are.
(13) The biographies of Jesus found in the Bible are in this class.
(14) Biographers may claim that their account is the "authentic" one.
(15) In advancing this claim, they are helped if the biography is "authorized" by the subject, this presumably allows the biographer special access to private information.
(16) "Unauthorized" biographies also have their appeal, however, since they can suggest an independence of mind in the biographer.
(17) In book promotions, the "unauthorized" characterisation usually suggests the prospect of juicy gossip that the subject had hoped to suppress.
(18) A subject might have several biographies, even several "authentic" ones.
(19) We sense intuitively that no one is in a position to tell the story of a life, perhaps not even the subject, and this has been proved by the history of biography.

Answer: Inference

a|true|college578.txt.passage:
(1) Governments around the world are struggling to cope with the rising cost of health care, and of drugs in particular.
(2) Many rich countries have resorted to price controls, and some on the American left advocate them noisily.
(3) But drug firms maintain that America, where they are free to price patented pills largely as they please, is the engine of global pharmaceutical innovation, while price-controlling Europeans are free riders.
(4) That, says PhRMA, the industry's lobby based in Washington, D.C., is because price regulations seen in other rich countries" chill innovation, impede patients' access to the newest cutting-edge medicines, and trigger innovators to relocate to countries with more progressive public policy."
(5) A new study, written by Donald Light, a visiting professor at Stanford University, claims that European drug firms are more innovative than American ones, in spite of price controls.
(6) That flies in the face of an influential paper published in the same journal in 2006, which examined the geographic origins of drugs registered between 1982 and 2003 and concluded that favourable public policies had helped propel America to the top of the list.
(7) PhRMA has rushed to denounce Mr Light's study, insisting it gives a" distorted picture"  and understates the impact of" home-grown innovation" .
(8) The tiff exposes two fallacies that come from looking at a globalised industry through national spectacles.
(9) Both sides attribute new drugs to a specific country of origin (based on the location of the headquarters of the firm that first launches them).
(10) But Patricia Danzon of the Wharton Business School argues that this makes little sense when most big drug firms have laboratories in several countries and often acquire drugs under development from biotechnology firms located elsewhere.
(11) By the same token, firms develop drugs for the global market, observes Alan Garber of Stanford, not just for the local one.
(12) So the imposition of price caps in a given country will not necessarily quash innovation there, thanks to the lure of exports.
(13) The exception to this rule, of course, is the United States, which alone accounts for over 40% of global sales.
(14) So what would happen if America's Congress imposes price regulations?
(15) If the cuts are swinging, then the prize for inventors everywhere will be reduced.
(16) But short of such a radical scenario, pricing reform would not kill off innovation, according to Sanford Bernstein, a financial-research firm.
(17) It has analysed the likely impact of a cut of 20% in the prices Medicare, the government health scheme for the elderly, pays for its drugs.
(18) It concluded that the earnings per share of big pharmaceutical firms would drop by 3-8%.

a|true|college543.txt.passage:
(1) The American military tribunals set up to pass judgment on terrorism suspects at Guantánamo Bay lurched into life this week when David Hicks, the" Australian Taliban" , unexpectedly pleaded guilty.
(2) The Pentagon will be relieved that the tribunals have started to show results after five years of controversy over the status of" enemy combatants" , claims of torture, the admissibility of forced confessions and a Supreme Court ruling last year that halted an earlier version of the tribunals.
(3) Yet the Hicks case is hardly an impressive start for America's offshore justice.
(4) Critics say the 31-year-old Mr Hicks, a former kangaroo skinner and alleged al-Qaeda fighter, faces a" kangaroo court" .
(5) Two of his civilian lawyers were barred from the hearing on March 26th.
(6) His military lawyer, Major Michael Mori, has been threatened with prosecution for harshly criticising the tribunals.
(7) The prosecutor, Colonel Morris Davis, said the defence lawyer may have breached military law that bans officers from using" contemptuous words"  against the president or senior officials.
(8) Major Mori filed a counter-charge against the prosecution, saying it was trying to intimidate him.
(9) In the end the motion will not be heard because Mr Hicks, looking pale and bedraggled, admitted the charge of" providing material support for terrorism" .
(10) A charge of" attempted murder in violation of the laws of war"  was dropped.
(11) As The Economist went to press, the tribunal was due to hear details of his plea and pass sentence, which Mr Hicks is expected to serve out in Australia.
(12) His father, Terry Hicks, said his son had had" five years of absolute hell"  since being captured in Afghanistan and allegedly suffering beatings, rape and forced injections in American custody-accusations rejected by the Pentagon.
(13) The guilty plea was just" a way to get home" , said Mr Hicks's father.
(14) Many in Australia regard Mr Hicks as more of a lost soul than a dangerous terrorist.
(15) Indeed, his charge sheet portrays him as little more than an al-Qaeda foot-soldier, and a poor one at that.
(16) His jihadi CV is pitiful compared with the evidence being given by some of the 14" high value detainees"  belatedly brought to Guantánamo from CIA secret prisons in September.
(17) They include al-Qaeda's operational chief, Khalid Sheikh Mohammed, who in a closed hearing to determine whether he was an" enemy combatant"  earlier this month boasted that he had organised the September 11th attacks" from A to Z" , and 30 other plots.
(18) Meanwhile, Ahmed Ghalfan Ghailani and Waleed bin Atttash, from Tanzania and Yemen respectively, have admitted supplying equipment for the bombing of the American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in 1998.
(19) Many of these big names will face trial, and perhaps the death penalty.
(20) But the tribunals are expected to try only 60-80 of the more than 380 prisoners currently in Guantánamo.
(21) Over the years hundreds of detainees have been sent back to their countries, where some have been jailed and most have been released-and sometimes re-arrested.
(22) The Bush Administration says it wants to phase out and eventually close the jail.
(23) But it is unlikely to do so for some years, either because it lacks the evidence to prosecute detainees (even under the more lax military rules it is using), or because other countries are unwilling to take them back.

a|true|college561.txt.passage:
(1) There was a time when big-league university presidents really mattered.
(2) The New York Times covered their every move.
(3) Presidents, the real ones, sought their counsel.
(4) For Woodrow Wilson and Dwight Eisenhower, being head of Princeton and Columbia, respectively, was a stepping-stone to the White House.
(5) Today, though, the job of college president is less and less removed from that of the Avon lady (except the house calls are made to the doorsteps of wealthy alumni).
(6) Ruth Simmons, the newly installed president of Brown University and the first African American to lead an Ivy League school, is a throwback to the crusading campus leaders of old.
(7) She doesn't merely marshal funds; she invests them in the great educational causes of our day.
(8) With the more than $300 million she raised as president of Smith College from 1995 to 2001, Simmons established an engineering program  (the first at any women's school) and added seminars focused on public speaking to purge the ubiquitous" likes"  and" ums"  from the campus idiom.
(9) At a meeting to discuss the future of Smith's math department, one professor timidly requested two more discussion sections for his course.
(10) Her response:" Dream bigger."
(11) Her own dream was born in a sharecropper's shack in East Texas where there was no money for books or toys-she and her 11 siblings each got an apple, an orange and 10 nuts for Christmas.
(12) Though she was called nigger on her walk to school, entering the classroom, she says, " was like waking up."
(13) When Simmons won a scholarship to Dillard University, her high school teachers took up a collection so she'd have a coat.
(14) She went on to Harvard to earn a Ph.
(15) D. in Romance languages.
(16) Simmons has made diversity her No.
(17) 1 campus crusade.
(18) She nearly doubled the enrollment of black freshmen at Smith, largely by traveling to high schools in the nation's poorest ZIP codes to recruit.
(19) Concerned with the lives of minority students once they arrive at school, she has fought to ease the racial standoffs that plague so many campuses.
(20) At Smith she turned down a request by students to have race-specific dorms.
(21) In 1993, while vice provost at Princeton, she wrote a now famous report recommending that the university establish an office of conflict resolution to defuse racial misunderstandings before they boiled over.
(22) Her first task at Brown will be to heal one such rupture last spring after the student paper published an incendiary ad by conservative polemicist David Horowitz arguing that blacks economically benefited from slavery."
(23) There's no safe ground for anybody in race relations, but campuses, unlike any other institution in our society, provide the opportunity to cross racial lines,"  says Simmons. "
(24) And even if you're hurt, you can't walk away.
(25) You have to walk over that line."

(10) "they thought,"who are trying to send messages to other planets,or to us?So the news was not given to the newspaper.

(17) Pulsars are strong radio stars.
(18) They are the smallest but the heaviest stars we know at present.
(19) A handful of pulsar would weigh a few thousand tons.
(20) Their light-if they give much light-is too small for us to see.

a|true|college566.txt.passage:
(1) As long as her parents can remember, 13-year-old Katie Hart has been talking about going to college.
(2) Her mother, Tally, a financial-aid officer at an Ohio university, knows all too well the daunting calculus of paying for a college education.
(3) Last year the average yearly tuition at a private, four-year school climbed 5.
(4) 5 percent to more than $17,000.
(5) The Harts have started saving, and figure they can afford a public university without a problem.
(6) But what if Katie applies to Princeton  (she's threatening), where one year's tuition, room and board-almost $34,000 in 2002-will cost more than some luxury cars?
(7) Even a number cruncher like Tally admits it's a little scary, especially since she'll retire and Katie will go to college at around the same time.
(8) Paying for college has always been a humbling endeavor.
(9) The good news: last year students collected $74 billion in financial aid, the most ever.
(10) Most families pay less than full freight.
(11) Sixty percent of public-university students and three quarters of those at private colleges receive some form of financial aid-mostly, these days, in the form of loans.
(12) But those numbers are not as encouraging as they appear for lower-income families, because schools are changing their formulas for distributing aid.
(13) Eager to boost their magazine rankings, which are based in part on the test scores of entering freshmen, they're throwing more aid at smarter kids-whether they need it or not.
(14) The best way to prepare is to start saving early.
(15) A new law passed last year makes that easier for some families.
(16) So-called 529 plans allow parents to sock away funds in federal-tax-free-investment accounts, as long as the money is used for" qualified education expenses"  like tuition, room and board.
(17) The plans aren't for everyone.
(18) For tax reasons, some lower-and middle-income families may be better off choosing other investments.
(19) But saving is vital.
(20) When's the best time to start?"
(21) Sometime,"  says Jack Joyce of the College Board, " between the maternity ward and middle school."
(22) Aid packages usually come in some combination of grants, loans and jobs.
(23) These days 60 percent of all aid comes in the form of low-interest loans.
(24) All students are eligible for" unsubsidized"  federal Stafford loans, which let them defer interest payments until after graduation.
(25) Students who can demonstrate need can also qualify for federal Perkins loans or" subsidized"  Staffords, where the government pays the interest during school.
(26) Fortunately, this is a borrower's market. "
(27) Interest rates are at their lowest level in the history of student loans,"  says Mark Kantrowitz, publisher of Finaid.
(28) Kantrowitz expects rates to fall even further when they're reviewed this summer.
(29) Traditional scholarships, academic or athletic, are still a part of many families' planning.
(30) Mack Reiter, a 17-year-old national wrestling champion, gets so many recruiting letters he throws most away.
(31) He'll almost certainly get a free ride.
(32) Without it, " we would really be in a bind,"  says his mother, Janet.
(33) For everyone else, it's worth the effort to pick through local and national scholarship offerings, which can be found on Web sites like collegeboard.
(34) com.
